ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for spiced cumin potato curry; A00LC (Potato products and dishes) is the closest defensible parent category.

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical restaurant serving of Jeera Aloo Haradhaniya is approximately 200 g, representing a side dish portion of spiced cumin potatoes garnished with fresh coriander.

nutrient_source.title: Aloo Jeera (Spiced Potato with Cumin) — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN reports nutrient values for Aloo Jeera (cumin-spiced potato preparation). Jeera Aloo Haradhaniya is the same dish with added fresh coriander garnish, which contributes negligible macronutrient change at typical garnish quantities. Values adapted from ICMR-NIN Aloo Jeera entry; coriander garnish effect on macros is negligible. Per-100g values reflect the cooked dish as served.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Jeera Aloo Haradhaniya. GI estimated at 54 based on boiled/cooked potato dishes with added fat and spices from the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al. 2021). Fat and spice content in Jeera Aloo reduces the GI of plain boiled potato (GI ~78) substantially; cooked spiced potato dishes with oil are consistently reported in the GI 50–58 range. GI 54 is used as a conservative mid-range estimate. GL per serving = (54 × 16.5 × 200/100) / 100 = 17.8.

hypertension.guidance: Jeera Aloo Haradhaniya contains approximately 210 mg sodium per 100 g (420 mg per typical 200 g serving), which represents a moderate sodium load; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size and request reduced-salt preparation when ordering from restaurants.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic index and carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a glycemic index of approximately 54 (low-moderate) and 16.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g, a standard 200 g serving delivers a glycemic load of approximately 17.8, which is moderate; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should monitor portion size and pair this dish with higher-fibre or lower-GI foods to blunt postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat and cholesterol-free profile
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Jeera Aloo Haradhaniya is prepared with vegetable oil and contains only 0.5 g saturated fat and 0 mg cholesterol per 100 g, making it a heart-friendly side dish; however, total fat intake from oil should be monitored in individuals following a calorie-restricted cardiac diet.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Jeera Aloo Haradhaniya is a classic North Indian dry potato curry made by tempering diced or cubed boiled potatoes in hot oil with cumin seeds (jeera), green chillies, turmeric, and other aromatic spices, then finishing with a generous garnish of fresh coriander leaves (haradhaniya). The dish is characterised by its earthy, nutty cumin aroma, mild heat, and the bright herbal freshness of coriander. The texture is soft and yielding from the potato with a lightly crisped exterior where the potato pieces have been sautéed in the tempered oil. It is a staple side dish across North India and is widely served in Indian restaurants in Singapore, including at establishments such as Kinara at Boat Quay. Nutritionally, the dish provides moderate carbohydrate energy from potato (approximately 16.5 g per 100 g), modest protein (2.1 g), and a small amount of fat from cooking oil (4.2 g), with a low-to-moderate glycemic index of approximately 54 due to the fat and spice matrix. It is naturally vegan, gluten-free, and dairy-free. Clinically, the dish is suitable for most dietary patterns in moderate portions; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should be mindful of the glycemic load (~17.8 per 200 g serving), and those with hypertension should note the sodium content (~210 mg per 100 g). The dish is rich in potassium (380 mg per 100 g) and provides useful amounts of vitamin C and B6 from the potato and coriander.

confidence_reason: No exact ICMR-NIN entry for Jeera Aloo Haradhaniya by that precise name; values adapted from ICMR-NIN Aloo Jeera (cumin potato) entry, which is the same dish without the explicit coriander garnish designation. The coriander garnish has negligible macronutrient impact. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for cooked spiced potato dishes. Confidence is medium rather than high due to the adapted rather than exact source match.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN Aloo Jeera data; the coriander (haradhaniya) garnish adds negligible macronutrient change at typical garnish quantities (5–10 g per serving). 2. selenium_mcg is set to -1 as selenium content for this dish is not reported in ICMR-NIN or the adapted source. 3. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 as this is a plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients; confirmed zero by food composition logic (naturally zero for vegan dishes). 4. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 as potatoes and plant-based spices contain negligible vitamin D; confirmed zero per ICMR-NIN and USDA data for this food type. 5. trans_fat_g is 0.0 as the dish is prepared with vegetable oil and contains no hydrogenated fats; confirmed zero by ingredient composition. 6. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 as the dish contains no animal-derived ingredients; naturally zero. 7. GI of 54 is an analogous estimate based on cooked spiced potato dishes with added fat from the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al. 2021, Am J Clin Nutr); no direct GI measurement for Jeera Aloo Haradhaniya was found in the approved GI database. 8. GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per 100g × serving_g / 100) / 100 = (54 × 16.5 × 200 / 100) / 100 = 17.82, rounded to 17.8. 9. FoodOn and FoodEx2 codes are closest-parent matches; no exact ontology code exists for this specific dish. 10. Source restaurant: Kinara - Boat Quay, Singapore (field survey data listing).
